text:
Finding a maximum independent set is a fundamental NP-hard problem with extensive real-world applications, requiring the identification of the largest subset of non-adjacent vertices in an undirected graph. In recent years, branch-and-bound and branch-and-reduce algorithms have emerged as the most effective exact solution methods. The branch-and-reduce framework, in particular, has successfully tackled previously intractable instances by integrating sophisticated reduction rules. However, while improvements in reduction techniques have driven recent progress, other critical algorithmic components—such as the branching strategy used to select the next vertex—have remained relatively under-explored. For years, the standard heuristic has simply been to branch on the vertex with the highest degree.

In this work, we introduce a Graph Neural Network (GNN) approach designed to optimize branching decisions. Given that the internal complexity of modern solvers makes traditional supervised or reinforcement learning difficult to implement, we employ a population-based genetic algorithm to evolve the model’s parameters. Our results demonstrate that this learned branching strategy outperforms traditional heuristics, achieving a speedup on 73% of benchmark instances with a median performance gain of 24%.
